content:
As someone from Gen Z, I genuinely understand the frustration and anger surrounding allegations of examination paper leaks. I appeared for CLAT UG, so I know the months of preparation, the sleepless nights, the sacrifices made by students and their families, and the emotional toll that uncertainty brings. Today, even while working in the private sector, I continue to empathize with every aspirant whose hard work deserves a fair opportunity.

Students have every right to demand transparency, accountability, and reforms. Peaceful protest and constructive criticism are the cornerstones of a healthy democracy.

However, what I fail to understand is why legitimate concerns are sometimes accompanied by abusive slogans, personal insults, or derogatory posters targeting the Hon'ble Prime Minister or any individual. When a movement shifts from demanding justice to promoting personal attacks, it risks weakening its own message.

The world is watching how we express our disagreements. The way we protest reflects not only our frustration but also our values as citizens. We can be firm without being disrespectful. We can ask difficult questions without resorting to abuse. A strong democracy is built on dialogue, accountability, and dignity not on hatred.

Let's raise our voices for a fair examination system, stronger safeguards against paper leaks, and accountability from every responsible authority. But let us also ensure that our methods reflect the maturity and integrity we expect from the institutions we seek to improve.

Justice demands courage. Change demands accountability. Both demand respect.
